To further improve robustness and generalization, I will add Mixup/CutMix and Exponential Moving Average (EMA). Mixup prevents memorization of noisy labels, and EMA provides a stable, smoothed model. I will increase training to 20 epochs since we have plenty of time (20 epochs should take ~35 mins). Decision: Update train.py with Mixup, EMA, and 20 epochs, then evaluate.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration used Mixup and CutMix with GCE loss, which resulted in a slight drop in accuracy (0.7808 vs 0.7838). Analysis: Mixup creates soft labels, which mathematically alters the behavior of the GCE loss and can reduce its effectiveness against label noise. To properly handle the noise, I will remove Mixup/CutMix, keep the EMA model (which helps with stability), and increase the GCE 'q' parameter to 0.8 for stronger noise robustness. I will train for 10 epochs. Decision: Rewrite train.py to remove Mixup, set q=0.8, and train for 10 epochs, then evaluate.

[eval_harness] Phase 2: Running evaluator
[eval_harness] Result: {'accuracy': 0.8119, 'balanced_accuracy': 0.8119}

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration with GCE loss (q=0.8) and EMA achieved 0.7536, which is lower than the 0.7838 achieved in Iteration 3 with GCE (q=0.7). Analysis: While GCE is robust, it still struggles with 40% noise. Since `cleanlab` is pre-installed, a more principled approach is to use Confident Learning to identify and remove the noisy labels, then train a final model on the clean subset. Decision: I will implement a two-phase approach. Phase 1: 5-fold cross-validation (3 epochs per fold with GCE loss) to get out-of-sample predicted probabilities for the entire training set. Phase 2: Use `cleanlab.filter.find_label_issues` to identify noisy samples and remove them. Phase 3: Train a final ConvNeXt-Tiny model on the clean subset for 15 epochs using Mixup and EMA. This should take ~55 minutes, well within the 122 minutes remaining budget.

[eval_harness] Phase 2: Running evaluator
[eval_harness] Result: {'accuracy': 0.8051, 'balanced_accuracy': 0.8051}

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration successfully used cleanlab to filter noisy labels and trained a ConvNeXt-Tiny model on the clean subset, achieving a new best accuracy of 0.8119. The iteration took ~70 minutes, leaving only ~49 minutes in the time budget. Analysis: We do not have enough time to run another full cleanlab pipeline or a long training session. However, we can squeeze out extra performance for free by using Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) and Ensembling. The workspace contains both `best.pt` (best standard model) and `best_ema.pt` (best Exponential Moving Average model). Decision: I will rewrite `predict.py` to load both models, apply Horizontal Flip TTA, and average their predictions. This requires zero training time and should provide a solid boost to the final accuracy. I will submit this for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: TTA + Ensemble in Iteration 7 dropped accuracy to 0.8051, meaning the standard EMA model from Iteration 6 (0.8119) is our best single model. Analysis: We have ~46 minutes left. We can use our highly accurate models (`best.pt` and `best_ema.pt`) to generate pseudo-labels and filter the training set. By keeping samples where the ensemble is highly confident (>80%) or agrees with the noisy label, we create a high-quality, relabeled dataset. Decision: Implement this pseudo-labeling strategy and train a new ConvNeXt-Tiny model for 18 epochs on the cleaned dataset. This should take ~30 minutes and potentially surpass the 0.8119 baseline.
